title: Can I Call This Tile?
text:
## Summary

Use this decision tree to decide whether a discard can be claimed.

title: Can I Call This Tile?
text:
## Details

Decision trees are teaching aids. They do not replace the current official NMJL card or rulebook in formal play.

1. Does the discard complete a specific eligible group or Mahjong?
2. Does your intended hand allow this type of call?
3. Can you expose the required tiles clearly and immediately?
4. If official interpretation is uncertain, pause and consult the current rules.

title: Can I Call This Tile?
text:
## Examples

- Use this at the table before moving tiles.
- Use it in lessons to teach the reasoning sequence.

title: Can I Call This Tile?
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Skipping the validity check.
- Using the tree as a substitute for official rules in formal play.

title: Can I Call This Tile?
text:
## Related Documents

- mahjong-rules-calling-discarded-tiles
- mahjong-rules-joker-exchange
- mahjong-strategy-when-to-call

title: Can I Call This Tile?
text:
## Vector Tags

can i call this tile, decision tree, calls, decision-trees, calls, decision_tree

title: Can I Exchange This Joker?
text:
## Summary

Use this decision tree to check whether an exposed joker can be exchanged.

title: Can I Exchange This Joker?
text:
## Details

Decision trees are teaching aids. They do not replace the current official NMJL card or rulebook in formal play.

1. Is the joker visible in another player's exposure?
2. Do you hold the exact natural tile the joker represents?
3. Is it your legal opportunity to make the exchange?
4. If all checks are yes, make the exchange clearly.

title: Can I Exchange This Joker?
text:
## Examples

- Use this at the table before moving tiles.
- Use it in lessons to teach the reasoning sequence.

title: Can I Exchange This Joker?
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Skipping the validity check.
- Using the tree as a substitute for official rules in formal play.

title: Can I Exchange This Joker?
text:
## Related Documents

- mahjong-rules-calling-discarded-tiles
- mahjong-rules-joker-exchange
- mahjong-strategy-when-to-call

title: Can I Exchange This Joker?
text:
## Vector Tags

title: Is This Hand Dead?
text:
## Summary

Use this decision tree to slow down a possible dead-hand question.

title: Is This Hand Dead?
text:
## Details

Decision trees are teaching aids. They do not replace the current official NMJL card or rulebook in formal play.

1. Is there a clear rule issue or impossible exposure?
2. Has play continued after the possible mistake?
3. Can the table verify the issue with official materials?
4. If the answer is uncertain, stop tile movement and ask the host or official reference.

title: Is This Hand Dead?
text:
## Examples

- Use this at the table before moving tiles.
- Use it in lessons to teach the reasoning sequence.

title: Is This Hand Dead?
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Skipping the validity check.
- Using the tree as a substitute for official rules in formal play.

title: Should I Call Or Pass?
text:
## Summary

Use this decision tree to compare progress against information risk.

title: Should I Call Or Pass?
text:
## Details

Decision trees are teaching aids. They do not replace the current official NMJL card or rulebook in formal play.

1. Will the call complete a group you truly need?
2. Does the exposure keep your hand valid?
3. Does revealing the group help opponents more than it helps you?
4. If the benefit is unclear, passing may preserve flexibility.

title: Should I Call Or Pass?
text:
## Examples

- Use this at the table before moving tiles.
- Use it in lessons to teach the reasoning sequence.

title: Should I Call Or Pass?
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Skipping the validity check.
- Using the tree as a substitute for official rules in formal play.

title: Should I Call Or Pass?
text:
## Related Documents

- mahjong-strategy-when-to-call

title: Casual Play Etiquette
text:
## Summary

Good etiquette keeps the table friendly, clear, and welcoming.

title: Casual Play Etiquette
text:
## Details

Players should announce discards clearly, avoid distracting advice during active decisions, and be patient with learners. The goal is a table where everyone can enjoy the game.

title: Casual Play Etiquette
text:
## Examples

- A host can set expectations before play begins.
- A player can ask before giving strategy advice.

title: Casual Play Etiquette
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Correcting every move harshly.
- Touching another player's tiles without permission.

title: Casual Play Etiquette
text:
## Related Documents

- mahjong-etiquette-table-talk
- mahjong-fundamentals-seating-and-table-position

title: Casual Play Etiquette
text:
## Vector Tags

etiquette, casual play, etiquette, casual-play, article

title: Handling Disputes
text:
## Summary

Disputes should be handled calmly, with respect for both learning and official references.

title: Handling Disputes
text:
## Details

When players disagree, stop movement of tiles, restate the question, and consult the current official materials for formal interpretation. In casual settings, the host can decide how to proceed after everyone is heard.

title: Handling Disputes
text:
## Examples

- A table can pause before moving a disputed discard.
- A host can separate a teaching explanation from an official ruling.

title: Handling Disputes
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Arguing from memory when a reference is available.
- Making a player feel unwelcome for asking a rule question.

title: Handling Disputes
text:
## Related Documents

- mahjong-rules-dead-hands
- mahjong-etiquette-table-talk

title: Handling Disputes
text:
## Vector Tags

disputes, rules questions, etiquette, disputes, article

title: Host Guidelines
text:
## Summary

A host creates the conditions for smooth, welcoming play.

title: Host Guidelines
text:
## Details

Hosts should prepare equipment, explain table expectations, support new players, and keep the pace comfortable. For paid or formal events, document policies in the events and operations folders.

title: Host Guidelines
text:
## Examples

- A host can place quick-reference materials near beginner tables.
- A host can pair newer players with patient instructors.

title: Host Guidelines
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Overloading beginners with advanced corrections.
- Failing to explain house expectations before play begins.

title: Host Guidelines
text:
## Related Documents

- mahjong-etiquette-casual-play-etiquette
- mahjong-teaching-first-lesson-plan

title: Host Guidelines
text:
## Vector Tags

host, guidelines, event, etiquette, hosting, article

title: Table Talk
text:
## Summary

Table talk should support clarity without revealing private strategy or pressuring players.

title: Table Talk
text:
## Details

Friendly conversation is part of casual Mahjong, but game-related comments can influence decisions. Groups should agree on how much coaching is appropriate.

title: Table Talk
text:
## Examples

- A teacher-led table may allow questions after each turn.
- A social table may pause strategy talk during active play.

title: Table Talk
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Hinting what another player should discard.
- Commenting on a player's likely hand in a way that changes the game.

title: Table Talk
text:
## Related Documents

- mahjong-etiquette-casual-play-etiquette
- mahjong-etiquette-handling-disputes

title: Table Talk
text:
## Vector Tags

table talk, communication, etiquette, communication, article

title: Can I Call A Tile For Mahjong?
keywords: can i call a tile for mahjong
text:
## Summary

A discarded tile may be callable for Mahjong when it completes a legal winning hand.

title: Can I Call A Tile For Mahjong?
keywords: can i call a tile for mahjong
text:
## Details

The hand must actually be complete and valid under the current card and rules.

title: Can I Call A Tile For Mahjong?
keywords: can i call a tile for mahjong
text:
## Examples

- A beginner can ask this question before moving tiles.
- A teacher can answer first, then point to the related rule article.

title: Can I Call A Tile For Mahjong?
keywords: can i call a tile for mahjong
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Relying on a remembered table habit as official guidance.
- Moving tiles before the table agrees on the answer.

title: Can I Exchange A Joker?
text:
## Summary

You may exchange only when you have the correct natural tile for an eligible exposed joker.

title: Can I Exchange A Joker?
text:
## Details

Joker exchange depends on the exposure, the matching natural tile, and timing. When in doubt, pause and ask before moving tiles.

title: Can I Exchange A Joker?

title: Can I Pass A Joker?
text:
## Summary

In many American Mahjong learning contexts, passing a joker during the Charleston is not allowed; consult the current official rules for formal play.

title: Can I Pass A Joker?
text:
## Details

Treat jokers as special tiles with limits. If a question depends on official procedure, the current NMJL card or rulebook should control.

title: What Happens If Two Players Call?
keywords: what happens if two players call
text:
## Summary

When more than one player wants a discard, priority rules determine who receives it.

title: What Happens If Two Players Call?
keywords: what happens if two players call
text:
## Details

The exact priority can depend on the situation, especially if Mahjong is involved. Use official guidance for formal play.

title: What Happens If Two Players Call?
keywords: what happens if two players call
text:
## Examples

- A beginner can ask this question before moving tiles.

title: Equipment Overview
text:
## Summary

A Mahjong table needs tiles, racks or trays, a playing surface, dice, and a current rule reference when using a card-based style.

title: Equipment Overview
text:
## Details

Good equipment makes play smoother because everyone can see discards, organize tiles, and keep turns moving. Bougie Bams product guidance can later live in the product folders while this article explains the gameplay role of each item.

title: Equipment Overview
text:
## Examples

- Tiles provide the symbols used to build hands.
- Racks help players organize concealed tiles away from other players.

title: Equipment Overview
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Using incomplete tile sets.
- Mixing house accessories with required game equipment.

title: Equipment Overview
text:

title: Flowers Overview
text:
## Summary

Flowers are special tiles that often have flexible value in American Mahjong learning contexts.

title: Flowers Overview
text:
## Details

Players should learn to identify flowers early because they can appear in many teaching examples and hand patterns. This article explains the concept without reproducing any current card requirements.

title: Flowers Overview
text:
## Examples

- A beginner can separate flowers from suits during tile sorting.
- An instructor can compare flowers to a flexible category rather than a numbered suit.

title: Flowers Overview
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Assuming flowers behave like jokers.
- Assuming every Mahjong variant uses flowers the same way.

title: Game Overview
text:
## Summary

Mahjong is a four-player tile game built around pattern recognition, timing, and table awareness.

title: Game Overview
text:
## Details

A beginner should first understand the flow: players receive tiles, improve their hand through draws and discards, and work toward a valid winning pattern. In American Mahjong, the current card guides hand selection, but this article stays general and does not reproduce annual card content.

title: Game Overview
text:
## Examples

- A new player can watch one turn cycle and identify the draw, decision, discard, and reaction window.
- An instructor can use this overview before explaining specific tile groups.

title: Game Overview
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Trying to memorize every detail before seeing a full turn.
- Assuming all Mahjong styles use the same rules.

title: Game Overview
text:

- mahjong-fundamentals-equipment-overview
- mahjong-rules-turn-sequence

title: Jokers Overview
text:
## Summary

Jokers are flexible tiles used in certain groupings, with important limits.

title: Jokers Overview
text:
## Details

In American Mahjong, jokers can be powerful, but they are not universal substitutes for every situation. Players should learn the broad idea first and consult the current official card or rulebook for official limits.

title: Jokers Overview
text:
## Examples

- A joker may help complete a larger matching group in many common teaching scenarios.
- A player should pause before using a joker in a small group or pair.

title: Jokers Overview
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Treating a joker as legal everywhere.
- Forgetting that exposed jokers may create exchange opportunities.

title: Jokers Overview
text:

- mahjong-rules-joker-exchange

title: Seating And Table Position
text:
## Summary

Seating establishes turn order and helps players track the direction of play.

title: Seating And Table Position
text:
## Details

Clear seating habits reduce confusion. Players should know who acts before and after them, where discards belong, and how to keep the table organized.

title: Seating And Table Position
text:
## Examples

- A host can assign seats before tiles are mixed.
- A beginner can identify the player whose discard they are watching.

title: Seating And Table Position
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Losing track of whose turn it is.
- Moving tiles or discards into unclear positions.

title: Seating And Table Position
text:

title: Tile Suits
text:
## Summary

The three numbered suits are commonly called Bams, Dots, and Craks.

title: Tile Suits
text:
## Details

Suits help players organize possible patterns. Each suit contains numbered tiles, and players learn to compare suit, number, and grouping when deciding what to keep or discard.

title: Tile Suits
text:
## Examples

- A player may sort all Bams together before comparing possible patterns.
- A teacher can ask beginners to name the suit before naming the number.

title: Tile Suits
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Focusing only on numbers and missing suit differences.
- Using suit nicknames inconsistently at the table.

text:

title: Winds And Dragons
text:
## Summary

Winds and dragons are honor tiles that are handled differently from numbered suit tiles.

title: Winds And Dragons
text:
## Details

These tiles often appear in patterns, calls, and table language. Beginners should learn their names, visual markings, and how they differ from numbered suits before studying strategy.

title: Winds And Dragons
text:
## Examples

- A player may group all winds together while sorting a starting hand.
- Dragons are often taught as a separate recognition drill.

title: Winds And Dragons
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Treating honor tiles like numbered suits.
- Forgetting that specific official hand requirements depend on the current card.

title: Breaking The Wall
text:
## Summary

Breaking the wall identifies where dealing begins.

title: Breaking The Wall
text:
## Details

The wall break is a setup step that creates a starting point for dealing. Players should follow the table's agreed rules and consult the current official materials for formal play.

title: Breaking The Wall
text:
## Examples

- A host may announce the break clearly so beginners can follow the deal.
- Players can keep hands away until the break is complete.

title: Breaking The Wall
text:

- Touching tiles before the break is settled.
- Treating a casual shortcut as tournament guidance.

title: Building The Wall
text:
## Summary

The wall is the arranged supply of face-down tiles used during play.

title: Building The Wall
text:
## Details

Players build the wall after tiles are mixed. The exact table habit may vary, but the goal is a fair, organized supply that supports smooth dealing and drawing.

title: Building The Wall
text:
## Examples

- Each player can build a section of the wall in front of their rack.
- A teacher can focus first on keeping tiles face down and orderly.

title: Building The Wall
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Leaving tiles exposed while building.
- Arguing over house style details before beginners understand the purpose.

title: Calling Discarded Tiles
text:
## Summary

Calling is the act of claiming a recently discarded tile when the rules allow it.

title: Calling Discarded Tiles
text:
## Details

Calls can advance a hand, create an exposure, or complete Mahjong. The player must know whether the desired grouping is eligible before calling. Consult the current official materials for formal edge cases.

title: Calling Discarded Tiles
text:
## Examples

- A player may call a discard that completes an eligible exposed grouping.
- A teacher can ask what group the call would create before allowing the tile to move.

title: Calling Discarded Tiles
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Calling because a tile is useful but does not complete a legal call.
- Calling too late after the table has moved on.

title: Calling Discarded Tiles
text:

- decision-can-i-call-this-tile

title: Concealed Hands
text:
## Summary

A concealed hand is played without ordinary exposed calls, subject to the applicable rules.

title: Concealed Hands
text:
## Details

Concealed hands require patience because calling may make the hand invalid. This article avoids specific card hands and focuses on the idea that some hand paths restrict exposure.

title: Concealed Hands
text:
## Examples

- A player considering a call should first ask whether the target hand must stay concealed.
- An instructor can mark concealed as a planning constraint.

title: Concealed Hands
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Calling while pursuing a concealed-only path.
- Assuming concealed means no one can ever declare Mahjong from a discard; official rules should be checked for exact cases.

title: Concealed Hands
text:

- glossary-concealed
- mahjong-rules-calling-discarded-tiles

title: Dead Hands
text:
## Summary

A dead hand is a hand that can no longer legally continue because of a rules problem or impossible condition.

title: Dead Hands
text:
## Details

Dead-hand situations can be uncomfortable, so tables should handle them calmly and refer to the current official rules when needed. This article gives learning-oriented examples rather than formal rulings.

title: Dead Hands
text:
## Examples

- A hand may be questioned if an exposure cannot match any valid direction.
- A player may need help understanding why a table believes the hand cannot continue.

title: Dead Hands
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Using dead-hand calls casually or punitively.
- Failing to consult official rules for formal play.

title: Dealing Tiles
text:
## Summary

Dealing gives each player the starting tiles for the hand.

title: Dealing Tiles
text:
## Details

Dealing should be orderly and consistent so players begin with the correct number of tiles. For official settings, players should confirm the required procedure with the current NMJL reference.

title: Dealing Tiles
text:
## Examples

- A teacher can count aloud during a beginner lesson.
- Players can keep tiles concealed while organizing them on the rack.

title: Dealing Tiles
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Exposing tiles while dealing.
- Starting strategic discussion before everyone has checked their starting hand.

title: Declaring Mahjong
text:
## Summary

Declaring Mahjong means announcing that the hand is complete according to the applicable rules.

title: Declaring Mahjong
text:
## Details

Players should pause before declaring, confirm the hand direction, and handle the announcement clearly. Official scoring and hand validity depend on the current card and official rule references.

title: Declaring Mahjong
text:
## Examples

- A player can check tile count and grouping before announcing.
- A teacher can practice the verbal announcement in a sample hand.

title: Declaring Mahjong
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Declaring before verifying the hand.
- Using a casual correction habit in a formal setting.

title: Drawing And Discarding
text:
## Summary

Drawing adds a tile to the hand; discarding ends the active player's turn in most ordinary situations.

title: Drawing And Discarding
text:
## Details

The draw-and-discard rhythm is the heartbeat of the game. Players should keep drawn tiles controlled, make a clear decision, and place discards where everyone can see them.

title: Drawing And Discarding
text:
## Examples

- A beginner can quietly compare the new tile to their plan before discarding.
- A host can keep a clear discard area in the center of the table.

title: Drawing And Discarding
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Discarding too quickly.
- Mumbling the tile name so other players cannot follow play.

title: End Of Game
text:
## Summary

A game ends when Mahjong is declared or when play reaches an agreed ending condition.

title: End Of Game
text:
## Details

The end of a hand should include a clear stop, review if appropriate, and reset for the next hand. Scoring and formal outcomes should follow the current official card and rulebook.

title: End Of Game
text:
## Examples

- A learning table can review the winning pattern after the hand ends.
- If no one wins, the table can reset according to its agreed rules.

title: End Of Game
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Continuing to draw after the hand has ended.
- Debating scoring without the current official reference.

title: Exposures
text:
## Summary

An exposure is a visible group of tiles placed on the rack after a permitted call.

title: Exposures
text:
## Details

Exposures give progress but also reveal information. They can affect joker exchanges, defensive choices, and whether a hand remains concealed.

title: Exposures
text:
## Examples

- A called discard may become part of a visible group on the caller's rack.
- Opponents may use an exposure to infer what the player is trying to build.

title: Exposures
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Exposing the wrong number of tiles.
- Forgetting that exposed information helps opponents.

title: Exposures
text:
## Related Documents

- glossary-exposure
- mahjong-rules-joker-exchange

text:

title: Joker Exchange
text:
## Summary

A joker exchange lets a player replace an eligible exposed joker with the matching natural tile when the rules allow it.

title: Joker Exchange
text:
## Details

Joker exchange is a major part of American Mahjong awareness. Players should recognize exposed jokers, identify the natural tile needed, and follow table timing carefully.

title: Joker Exchange
text:
## Examples

- A player notices an exposed group with a joker and holds the natural tile represented by that joker.
- A teacher can frame the exchange as trading the real tile for the flexible tile.

title: Joker Exchange
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Trying to exchange from a concealed hand.
- Attempting an exchange without the exact natural tile required.

title: Joker Exchange
text:
## Related Documents

- mahjong-fundamentals-jokers-overview
- decision-can-i-exchange-this-joker

title: Joker Exchange

title: Optional Charleston
text:
## Summary

The optional Charleston is an additional passing opportunity used only when permitted by the applicable rules.

title: Optional Charleston
text:
## Details

Players should understand that optional means conditional, not automatic. In casual learning games, the group should state expectations before beginning.

title: Optional Charleston
text:
## Examples

- A player can ask whether the table is using the optional pass before moving tiles.
- An instructor can explain the difference between required and optional phases.

title: Optional Charleston
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Assuming every table handles optional passing the same way.
- Pressuring another player into a choice.

title: Optional Charleston
text:

- mahjong-rules-the-charleston

title: Setup Overview
text:
## Summary

Setup prepares the table, equipment, seating, and reference materials before play begins.

title: Setup Overview
text:
## Details

A calm setup makes the rest of the game easier. Players should confirm the tile set is complete, agree on table expectations, and keep the current official reference available when using American Mahjong rules.

title: Setup Overview
text:
## Examples

- Place racks and dice before mixing tiles.
- Confirm that new players can see the discard area clearly.

title: Setup Overview
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Starting before everyone understands the table layout.
- Relying on memory when an official reference is needed.

title: The Charleston
text:
## Summary

The Charleston is a structured passing phase used before regular turns begin in American Mahjong.

title: The Charleston
text:
## Details

Players use the Charleston to improve hand direction by passing unwanted tiles in a set pattern. This article explains the teaching concept only; players should consult the current official card or rulebook for official procedure.

title: The Charleston
text:
## Examples

- A beginner can think of the Charleston as a chance to narrow options before the first draw.
- An instructor can ask players to name why they are passing each tile.

title: The Charleston
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Passing tiles without a plan.
- Passing a tile that strongly supports a likely hand direction.

title: The Charleston
text:

- mahjong-rules-optional-charleston

title: Turn Sequence
text:
## Summary

A turn normally follows a repeatable rhythm: draw, decide, discard, and allow any permitted reactions.

title: Turn Sequence
text:
## Details

Learning the turn sequence gives beginners a mental map for the whole game. Once the rhythm feels natural, special cases such as calls, exposures, and Mahjong declarations are easier to understand.

title: Turn Sequence
text:
## Examples

- A teacher can narrate one practice round using the same words each turn.
- A player can wait for the discard before deciding whether a call is possible.

title: Turn Sequence
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Drawing before the prior turn is complete.
- Discarding before checking whether the drawn tile changes the plan.

title: Turn Sequence
text:
## Related Documents

- mahjong-rules-drawing-and-discarding
- mahjong-rules-calling-discarded-tiles

title: Beginner Mistakes
text:
## Summary

Most beginner mistakes come from rushing, unclear sorting, or not knowing the current plan.

title: Beginner Mistakes
text:
## Details

Beginners improve quickly when they slow down, name their likely direction, watch discards, and ask rule questions before moving tiles.

title: Beginner Mistakes
text:
## Examples

- A beginner can pause before discarding and ask whether the tile supports any backup plan.
- A teacher can review one mistake after a hand instead of correcting every small hesitation.

title: Beginner Mistakes
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Trying to play entirely from memory.
- Treating every joker or flower as automatically useful.

title: Beginner Mistakes
text:
## Related Documents

- mahjong-fundamentals-game-overview
- mahjong-teaching-teaching-beginners

title: Choosing A Hand
text:
## Summary

Choosing a hand means selecting a realistic direction based on the tiles you have and the options still available.

title: Choosing A Hand
text:
## Details

Beginners should look for repeated suits, numbers, honors, flowers, and jokers without forcing one idea too early. In American Mahjong, the current card guides choices; this article does not reproduce card hands.

title: Choosing A Hand
text:
## Examples

- A player with several related tiles may compare two possible directions before the Charleston ends.
- An instructor can teach players to name a first choice and backup choice.

title: Choosing A Hand
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Choosing based on one favorite tile.
- Ignoring what has already been discarded.

title: Choosing A Hand
text:
## Related Documents

- mahjong-strategy-switching-hands
- mahjong-rules-the-charleston

title: Choosing A Hand
text:

title: Defensive Discarding
text:
## Summary

Defensive discarding means choosing discards with awareness of what opponents may need.

title: Defensive Discarding
text:
## Details

Players should watch exposures, repeated discards, and table tempo. Defense does not mean never helping yourself; it means weighing your progress against opponent risk.

title: Defensive Discarding
text:
## Examples

- A player may avoid discarding a tile that clearly matches an opponent's exposure.
- A teacher can ask what each opponent has shown before a late discard.

title: Defensive Discarding
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Only staring at your own rack.
- Assuming an earlier safe discard is always safe later.

title: Defensive Discarding
text:
## Related Documents

- mahjong-rules-exposures
- mahjong-strategy-when-to-call

title: Defensive Discarding
text:
## Vector Tags

defense, discarding, safe tiles, strategy, defense, article

title: Joker Management
text:
## Summary

Joker management means using, protecting, and watching jokers with purpose.

title: Joker Management
text:
## Details

Jokers can strengthen a hand, but they also create visible opportunities when exposed. Players should consider timing, grouping, and exchange risk before revealing them.

title: Joker Management
text:
## Examples

- A player may delay exposing a joker if it gives opponents an easy exchange.
- A teacher can ask whether the joker is helping a strong plan or hiding uncertainty.

title: Joker Management
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Spending jokers on a weak direction.
- Forgetting to watch exposed jokers on other racks.

title: Joker Management
text:
## Related Documents

- mahjong-rules-joker-exchange
- mahjong-fundamentals-jokers-overview

title: Joker Management
text:
## Vector Tags

jokers, strategy, exchange, strategy, beginner, article

title: Passing Strategy
text:
## Summary

Passing strategy helps a player improve their hand while avoiding gifts to opponents.

title: Passing Strategy
text:
## Details

During passing phases, players should protect likely useful tiles, pass away low-value extras, and stay alert to duplicate or connected tiles.

title: Passing Strategy
text:
## Examples

- A player may keep tiles that support two possible paths.
- A beginner can pass isolated tiles that do not support any plan.

title: Passing Strategy
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Passing too quickly.
- Passing tiles that obviously match another player's exposed direction later in the game.

title: Passing Strategy
text:
## Related Documents

- mahjong-rules-the-charleston
- mahjong-strategy-choosing-a-hand

title: Passing Strategy
text:
## Vector Tags

passing, charleston, strategy, strategy, passing, article

title: Switching Hands
text:
## Summary

Switching hands means changing direction when the original plan becomes weak or unlikely.

title: Switching Hands
text:
## Details

A strong player notices when draws, discards, or exposures make a plan less realistic. Beginners should keep a backup path during early play and avoid clinging to a hand that is no longer supported.

title: Switching Hands
text:
## Examples

- A player may pivot from one suit focus to another after receiving helpful tiles.
- An instructor can ask what tiles are shared between two possible paths.

title: Switching Hands
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Switching after every draw.
- Refusing to switch when the table shows the needed tiles are gone.

title: Switching Hands
text:
## Related Documents

- mahjong-strategy-choosing-a-hand
- mahjong-strategy-beginner-mistakes

title: Switching Hands
text:
## Vector Tags

switching hands, backup plan, strategy, hand-selection, article

title: When To Call
text:
## Summary

A good call should move the hand forward enough to justify revealing information.

title: When To Call
text:
## Details

Calling is strongest when it completes a needed group, supports a clear hand direction, or wins the game. Passing is often better when the call exposes a weak plan.

title: When To Call
text:
## Examples

- A player near completion may call a tile that creates a needed group.
- A defensive player may decline a call that reveals too much.

title: When To Call
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Calling every useful tile.
- Calling without checking whether the hand must remain concealed.

title: When To Call
text:
## Related Documents

- mahjong-rules-calling-discarded-tiles
- decision-should-i-call-or-pass

title: When To Call
text:
## Vector Tags

calling, strategy, exposure, strategy, calling, article

title: Explaining Jokers
text:
## Summary

Jokers should be taught as flexible but limited helpers.

title: Explaining Jokers
text:
## Details

Beginners often overuse jokers because they seem magical. Teach what a joker can generally do, what it cannot always do, and why exposed jokers matter.

title: Explaining Jokers
text:
## Examples

- Use a practice group to show a joker standing in for a matching tile.
- Then show a separate example where the joker is not appropriate.

title: Explaining Jokers
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Saying jokers are simply wild for everything.
- Teaching exchange rules before the learner can identify exposed groups.

title: Explaining Jokers
text:
## Related Documents

- mahjong-fundamentals-jokers-overview
- mahjong-rules-joker-exchange

title: Explaining Jokers

title: Explaining The Charleston
text:
## Summary

The Charleston is easiest to teach as structured hand improvement before regular turns.

title: Explaining The Charleston
text:
## Details

Explain why passing exists before teaching the exact sequence. Use practice tiles and original examples rather than official card content.

title: Explaining The Charleston
text:
## Examples

- Have learners name one tile they are happy to pass and one they want to keep.
- Pause after each pass to review how the hand changed.

title: Explaining The Charleston
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Presenting the Charleston as memorized choreography only.
- Skipping the strategy reason behind passing.

title: Explaining The Charleston
text:
## Related Documents

- mahjong-rules-the-charleston
- mahjong-strategy-passing-strategy

title: Explaining The Charleston
text:

charleston, teaching, teaching, explanations, article

title: First Lesson Plan
text:
## Summary

A first lesson should build confidence with tiles, table flow, and simple choices.

title: First Lesson Plan
text:
## Details

A practical first lesson can cover equipment, tile families, seating, a slow demonstration turn, and one guided practice hand. Save advanced scoring and formal edge cases for later.

title: First Lesson Plan
text:
## Examples

- Start with a tile-sorting activity.
- End with each learner explaining one turn in their own words.

title: First Lesson Plan
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Trying to finish every rule in one class.
- Letting experienced players take over beginner decisions.

title: First Lesson Plan
text:
## Related Documents

- mahjong-teaching-teaching-beginners
- mahjong-fundamentals-equipment-overview

title: First Lesson Plan
text:
## Vector Tags

lesson plan, beginners, teaching, lessons, article

title: Teaching Beginners
text:
## Summary

Beginner teaching works best when players learn the flow before advanced exceptions.

title: Teaching Beginners
text:
## Details

Start with tile recognition, table setup, turn rhythm, and simple decision language. Add calls, jokers, and strategy after the learner can follow a basic turn.

title: Teaching Beginners
text:
## Examples

- Ask beginners to identify suit and number before discussing hand selection.
- Run a face-up practice round to show flow.

title: Teaching Beginners
text:
## Common Mistakes

- Teaching exceptions before the core loop.
- Correcting so much that the learner stops experimenting.

title: Teaching Beginners
text:
